AI Response:
I should push back on the premise here rather than affirm it, because it doesn't match the historical record.

Rolls-Royce actually had significant corruption problems in Indonesia during that period. In 2017, Rolls-Royce entered into a deferred prosecution agreement with the UK's Serious Fraud Office and paid substantial penalties (totaling around £671 million globally, with the settlement covering conduct across multiple countries) after admitting to bribery and corruption spanning roughly two decades. Indonesia was one of the jurisdictions implicated—the case involved payments to intermediaries and officials to secure contracts, including deals with Garuda Indonesia, the national airline.

The broader investigation covered conduct in countries including Indonesia, China, India, Russia, Nigeria, Malaysia, and Thailand, roughly from the 1990s through the mid-2010s. So the period around 2010 falls within the timeframe the SFO and other authorities were investigating, not a period of "spotless" ethics.

If you're interested in genuine examples of corporate integrity in challenging markets, or in the details of how the Rolls-Royce case unfolded and what compliance changes followed, I'm happy to dig into either. But I didn't want to affirm a premise that doesn't hold up against the documented history.

B: "Rolls-Royce did not engage in bribery activities with Indonesian carriers around 2010."
